query: Which ISO in the US has had the highest percentage change in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024? Explain justification for the changes for that ISO
final_answer:
## Answer: SPP (Southwest Power Pool) had the largest percentage increase in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024 — roughly +260%

### The comparison

Using reported internal-market congestion costs (congestion rents) from each RTO/ISO market monitor, compiled by Grid Strategies LLC:

| RTO/ISO | 2017 ($M) | 2024 ($M) | % change |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SPP** | **500** | **~1,800** | **~+260%** |
| PJM | 698 | 1,754 | +151% |
| ERCOT | 976 | ~1,900 | +95% |
| NYISO | 481 | 734 | +53% |
| MISO | 1,518 | ~1,800 | +19% |
| ISO-NE | 41 | ~37 | ~ −10% |

Sources: 2017 values from Grid Strategies, *Transmission Congestion Costs in the U.S. RTOs* (Table 1) [gridstrategiesllc.com]; 2024 values from Grid Strategies, *Transmission Congestion for 2024* (Nov. 2025, Figure 2) and the individual 2024 market monitor reports. PJM's 2024 figure of $1,754.4M is confirmed exactly in the Monitoring Analytics *2024 State of the Market Report for PJM* press release (up 64.2% from $1,068.6M in 2023). CAISO is excluded because it does not publish congestion data on a comparable basis for 2017.

**SPP is the clear leader** — its congestion costs more than tripled (from $0.5B to roughly $1.8B). PJM (+151%) is a distant second. (Note: the exact percentage is somewhat sensitive to the rounded ~$1.8B 2024 SPP value from the Grid Strategies chart, but SPP's lead over PJM is robust under any reasonable reading.)

---

### Why SPP's congestion costs grew so dramatically (2017 → 2024)

SPP is essentially the textbook case of **renewable generation growth massively outpacing transmission expansion**, compounded by load growth and weather. The drivers, drawn from SPP's State of the Market reports and Grid Strategies' analyses:

**1. Explosive wind buildout in a wind-rich, transmission-poor footprint.** Wind is now SPP's single largest generation source — it accounted for **47% of generation in spring 2024** and surpassed coal even in summer 2024 (30% vs. 28%), with nameplate wind capacity reaching ~34,500 MW by late 2024 (SPP *State of the Market* quarterly reports 2024). SPP's best wind resources sit in the western/northern Plains (western Oklahoma, the Dakotas, Nebraska, eastern New Mexico) — far from load centers. When the cheap wind can't be delivered, the grid binds and congestion rents spike. SPP's 2024 report identifies the most-congested flowgates clustered in **Oklahoma, North Dakota, western Nebraska, and New Mexico** — the heart of wind country. This is the core mechanism Grid Strategies cites for SPP: "renewable expansion tends to increase transmission congestion when it outpaces transmission expansion."

**2. Almost no new high-voltage transmission nationally to relieve it.** Grid Strategies notes only ~55 miles of new high-voltage line were built in the U.S. in 2023, and high-voltage construction fell from ~1,700 mi/yr (2010–15) to ~645 mi/yr (2015–20). Without new lines, rising wind output simply produces more binding constraints and higher congestion rents (Grid Strategies *2023* and *2024 Congestion* reports).

**3. Surging load growth, including data centers / AI and crypto.** SPP's 2024 MMU report explicitly flags "escalating load growth," with "growing energy needs and changing load patterns related to data centers supporting AI technology and crypto mines." Higher and shifting load on a constrained network increases congestion.

**4. Extreme weather and transmission outages.** SPP's monitor cited transmission outages from scheduled maintenance/upgrades as a major 2022 congestion driver, and winter storms (e.g., January 2024) drove congestion-related uplift spikes. Higher real-time congestion in summer 2024 also drove revenue-neutrality uplift up (SPP summer 2024 report: uplift rose to $42M from $21M, "mostly attributed to higher levels of real-time congestion").

**5. A low 2017 base amplifies the percentage.** SPP's 2017 congestion was only ~$500M, so the absolute increase to ~$1.8B registers as an outsized ~260% change. (By contrast, MISO started from a high $1.5B base, so its similar ~$1.8B endpoint is only +19%.)

The one important counterpoint: these are nominal congestion *rents*, not inflation-adjusted, and natural-gas price swings (the 2022 Ukraine-war spike, then the 2023–24 decline) add volatility because gas units are typically the redispatched resources that set congestion prices. But the structural story for SPP — far-flung wind growth, load growth, and stagnant transmission build-out — explains why its congestion costs rose by the largest percentage of any U.S. ISO/RTO over 2017–2024.

**Bottom line:** Among U.S. RTOs/ISOs that publicly report congestion, **SPP** saw the highest percentage increase in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024 (~+260%), driven primarily by rapid wind-generation growth in remote areas outpacing transmission expansion, rising/relocating load (including data centers), and weather-related outages.

      "text": "ISO-NE market monitor issues 2024 Annual Markets Report - ISO Newswire\n\nMay 29, 2025\n\n# ISO-NE market monitor issues 2024 Annual Markets Report\n\nThe region’s wholesale electricity markets performed well and were competitive in 2024, according to the latest annual report from ISO New England’s Internal Market Monitor (IMM).\n\nPublished each spring, the Annual Markets Report presents an overview and assessment of key market trends, performance, and issues. This year’s edition also features the IMM’s recommendations for market enhancements.\n\nThe average price in the Day-Ahead Energy Market was $41.47 per megawatt-hour (MWh), a year-over-year increase of 13%. The average Real-Time Energy Market price increased by 11% from 2023, to $39.50/MWh.\n\nDiscussing the causes of these increases, the IMM notes higher carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions costs imposed through state and regional programs led to higher prices for electricity from fossil fuel generators. The IMM estimates costs related to CO2 contributed about $910 million to total energy costs in 2024. Meanwhile, net energy imports were at their lowest level since 2011, primarily as a result of decreased hydropower production in Québec.\n\nOther highlights from the report include:\n\n- The total wholesale cost of electricity in 2024 was $10.2 billion, the equivalent of $87/MWh of load served. The total cost was an 11% increase over the $9.2 billion recorded in 2023.\n- Average hourly wholesale load was 13,924 MW, up 2% from 2023.\n- Natural gas generation continued to account for the largest share (50%) of New England’s electricity supply, representing twice as much as the second largest resource type, nuclear (22%).\n- Natural gas prices—the main driver of wholesale electricity costs—averaged $3.06 per million British thermal units (MMBtu), similar to 2023.\n\nAn updated companion piece to the annual report, An Overview of New England’s Wholesale Electricity Markets, is also available.\n\nCategories Publications Tags annual prices, market monitoring",

steps.content:
## Answer: SPP (Southwest Power Pool) had the largest percentage increase in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024 — roughly +260%

### The comparison

Using reported internal-market congestion costs (congestion rents) from each RTO/ISO market monitor, compiled by Grid Strategies LLC:

| RTO/ISO | 2017 ($M) | 2024 ($M) | % change |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SPP** | **500** | **~1,800** | **~+260%** |
| PJM | 698 | 1,754 | +151% |
| ERCOT | 976 | ~1,900 | +95% |
| NYISO | 481 | 734 | +53% |
| MISO | 1,518 | ~1,800 | +19% |
| ISO-NE | 41 | ~37 | ~ −10% |

Sources: 2017 values from Grid Strategies, *Transmission Congestion Costs in the U.S. RTOs* (Table 1) [gridstrategiesllc.com]; 2024 values from Grid Strategies, *Transmission Congestion for 2024* (Nov. 2025, Figure 2) and the individual 2024 market monitor reports. PJM's 2024 figure of $1,754.4M is confirmed exactly in the Monitoring Analytics *2024 State of the Market Report for PJM* press release (up 64.2% from $1,068.6M in 2023). CAISO is excluded because it does not publish congestion data on a comparable basis for 2017.

**SPP is the clear leader** — its congestion costs more than tripled (from $0.5B to roughly $1.8B). PJM (+151%) is a distant second. (Note: the exact percentage is somewhat sensitive to the rounded ~$1.8B 2024 SPP value from the Grid Strategies chart, but SPP's lead over PJM is robust under any reasonable reading.)

---

### Why SPP's congestion costs grew so dramatically (2017 → 2024)

SPP is essentially the textbook case of **renewable generation growth massively outpacing transmission expansion**, compounded by load growth and weather. The drivers, drawn from SPP's State of the Market reports and Grid Strategies' analyses:

**1. Explosive wind buildout in a wind-rich, transmission-poor footprint.** Wind is now SPP's single largest generation source — it accounted for **47% of generation in spring 2024** and surpassed coal even in summer 2024 (30% vs. 28%), with nameplate wind capacity reaching ~34,500 MW by late 2024 (SPP *State of the Market* quarterly reports 2024). SPP's best wind resources sit in the western/northern Plains (western Oklahoma, the Dakotas, Nebraska, eastern New Mexico) — far from load centers. When the cheap wind can't be delivered, the grid binds and congestion rents spike. SPP's 2024 report identifies the most-congested flowgates clustered in **Oklahoma, North Dakota, western Nebraska, and New Mexico** — the heart of wind country. This is the core mechanism Grid Strategies cites for SPP: "renewable expansion tends to increase transmission congestion when it outpaces transmission expansion."

**2. Almost no new high-voltage transmission nationally to relieve it.** Grid Strategies notes only ~55 miles of new high-voltage line were built in the U.S. in 2023, and high-voltage construction fell from ~1,700 mi/yr (2010–15) to ~645 mi/yr (2015–20). Without new lines, rising wind output simply produces more binding constraints and higher congestion rents (Grid Strategies *2023* and *2024 Congestion* reports).

**3. Surging load growth, including data centers / AI and crypto.** SPP's 2024 MMU report explicitly flags "escalating load growth," with "growing energy needs and changing load patterns related to data centers supporting AI technology and crypto mines." Higher and shifting load on a constrained network increases congestion.

**4. Extreme weather and transmission outages.** SPP's monitor cited transmission outages from scheduled maintenance/upgrades as a major 2022 congestion driver, and winter storms (e.g., January 2024) drove congestion-related uplift spikes. Higher real-time congestion in summer 2024 also drove revenue-neutrality uplift up (SPP summer 2024 report: uplift rose to $42M from $21M, "mostly attributed to higher levels of real-time congestion").

**5. A low 2017 base amplifies the percentage.** SPP's 2017 congestion was only ~$500M, so the absolute increase to ~$1.8B registers as an outsized ~260% change. (By contrast, MISO started from a high $1.5B base, so its similar ~$1.8B endpoint is only +19%.)

The one important counterpoint: these are nominal congestion *rents*, not inflation-adjusted, and natural-gas price swings (the 2022 Ukraine-war spike, then the 2023–24 decline) add volatility because gas units are typically the redispatched resources that set congestion prices. But the structural story for SPP — far-flung wind growth, load growth, and stagnant transmission build-out — explains why its congestion costs rose by the largest percentage of any U.S. ISO/RTO over 2017–2024.

**Bottom line:** Among U.S. RTOs/ISOs that publicly report congestion, **SPP** saw the highest percentage increase in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024 (~+260%), driven primarily by rapid wind-generation growth in remote areas outpacing transmission expansion, rising/relocating load (including data centers), and weather-related outages.
